coredata.dc:title: Full-spectrum luminescence of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots and their applications in LED lighting and organelle-lysosome labeling
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jing
Zhao, Songfang
Guan, Ruifang
Zhang, Hao
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom doping can achieve specific regulation of carbon dots by changing their nanostructure, electronic structure and band gap. Here, we synthesized nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with full-spectrum fluorescence emission capability. Through comprehensive structural analysis using XPS, FTIR, and other characterization techniques, it was revealed that most of the nitrogen atoms were integrated into the carbon core of the N, S-CDs. Meanwhile, sulfur atoms were identified on the surface of the N, S-CDs, incorporated as functional groups. This unique structural configuration provides a foundation for understanding the solvent effect on N, S-CDs. Specifically, as the polarity of the solvent increases, the fluorescence emission of the N, S-CDs experiences a red-shift. The varying hydrogen donor abilities of different solvents contribute to changes in fluorescence emission. Notably, N, S-CDs exhibit near-infrared fluorescence emission when dissolved in acetic acid solvent. Under the synergistic effect of solvent polarity and hydrogen bonding, the fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs achieves full-spectrum luminescence, spanning from blue light to near-infrared. It is particularly interesting to observe that when N, S-CDs are dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films by dissolving them in three different solvents, the red, green, and yellow light emission can be achieved. When coated onto Light Emitting Diode (LED) lamp beads, these films exhibit versatile light emission properties. This provides inspiration for the development and preparation of LED fluorescent materials. Meanwhile, thanks to the excellent luminescent properties of N, S-CDs in water, they can be used for targeted localization of lysosomes in biological cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots
Solvent effects
LED light emission
Heme fluorescence probes
Targeted lysosome imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of N, S-CDs
The preparation of different solvent of N, S-CDs solutions
The preparation of N, S-CDs/PVA composite film
Cell imaging and colocalization assays of N, S-CDs
Basic optical properties of N, S-CDs
N, S-CDs/PVA composite materials and solid-state thin film luminescence
Hemoglobin fluorescent probes and lysosomal staining

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, S-CDs, inset: HRTEM image of N, S-CDs; (b) Size distribution of N, S-CDs; (c) FTIR spectrum of N, S-CDs (red line) and L-CDs (black line); (d) XPS spectrum of N, S-CDs (The illustration was percentage of C, O, N, S elements in N, S-CDs). High-resolution spectra of elements C1s (e), N1s (f), O1s (g), S 2p3 (h). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Normalized absorption spectrum, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum, and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum of N, S-CDs; (b) PL emission spectra of N, S-CDs under different excitation wavelengths; (c) PL emission spectra of N, S-CDs under different pH values; (d) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of N, S-CDs.

(a) Emission peaks of N, S-CDs dissolved in different solvents under 365 nm excitation (Upper illustration shows photographs of N, S-CDs in different solvents under 365 nm UV light. Solvents from left to right: n-Hex, EA, DMF, EtOH, FA, H2O, AA,), (b) Relationship between solvent polarity and the optimal fluorescence emission Wavelength of N, S-CDs. (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of N, S-CDs dissolved in seven different solvents (n-Hex, EA, DMF, EtOH, H2O, FA, AA).. (Upper illustration shows photographs of N, S-CDs in different solvents under daylight.)

(a) PVA film; (b) PVA/CDs composite film using 50 % AA as the solvent; (c) PVA/CDs composite film using 50 % FA as the solvent; (d) PVA/CDs composite film using 50 % DMF as the solvent; (e) Emission spectra of RLED, (f) YLED and (g) GLED (illustrations: Optical photographs of RLED, YLED and GLED) (h) CIE chromaticity diagrams for preparing RLED, HLED and GLED.

(a) Specific recognition of hemoglobin; (b) Fluorescence response of 20 μL hemoglobin added with and without different biomolecules; (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs at different hemoglobin concentrations; (d) Trend graph showing fluorescence intensity change versus hemoglobin addition amount. (e) Time-resolved decay curves of N, S-CDs aqueous solution (red) and N, S-CDs + Hemin. (f) UV absorption spectra of N, S-CDs (red), N, S-CDs + Hemin (black), and Hemin (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Confocal fluorescence images and confocal colocalization analysis of A549 cells incubated with N, S-CDs (80 μg mL−1) and LTR dye for 2 h: (a) Lysosomes (scale bar: 20 μm); (b) Deep red lipid droplets (scale bar: 20 μm); (c) Mitochondria (scale bar: 20 μm). (d) Confocal fluorescence images of A549 cells stimulated with heme at different concentrations.
Synthesis strategies and solvent-dependent luminescence of N, S-CDs.
Polarity Values of Different Solvents (Dielectric constant, EN T polarity parameter, and α, β, ᴨ∗ Kamlet-Taft polarity parameters of the solvents.).

head.ce:title: Full-spectrum luminescence of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots and their applications in LED lighting and organelle-lysosome labeling

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong, 250022, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong, 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom doping can achieve specific regulation of carbon dots by changing their nanostructure, electronic structure and band gap. Here, we synthesized nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) with full-spectrum fluorescence emission capability. Through comprehensive structural analysis using XPS, FTIR, and other characterization techniques, it was revealed that most of the nitrogen atoms were integrated into the carbon core of the N, S-CDs. Meanwhile, sulfur atoms were identified on the surface of the N, S-CDs, incorporated as functional groups. This unique structural configuration provides a foundation for understanding the solvent effect on N, S-CDs. Specifically, as the polarity of the solvent increases, the fluorescence emission of the N, S-CDs experiences a red-shift. The varying hydrogen donor abilities of different solvents contribute to changes in fluorescence emission. Notably, N, S-CDs exhibit near-infrared fluorescence emission when dissolved in acetic acid solvent. Under the synergistic effect of solvent polarity and hydrogen bonding, the fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs achieves full-spectrum luminescence, spanning from blue light to near-infrared. It is particularly interesting to observe that when N, S-CDs are dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films by dissolving them in three different solvents, the red, green, and yellow light emission can be achieved. When coated onto Light Emitting Diode (LED) lamp beads, these films exhibit versatile light emission properties. This provides inspiration for the development and preparation of LED fluorescent materials. Meanwhile, thanks to the excellent luminescent properties of N, S-CDs in water, they can be used for targeted localization of lysosomes in biological cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a promising nanomaterial within the “carbon family", represent a class of zero-dimensional carbon-based material with dimensions less than 10 nm [1]. Since their discovery in 2004, CDs have attracted widespread attention due to their low cost-effectiveness, exceptional water solubility and biocompatibility, as well as remarkable photoluminescence properties. A key challenge lies in effectively and directly enhancing the diverse properties of CDs [2,3]. In the synthesis process of CDs, the nature of the final product can be controlled by carefully selecting appropriate precursors and adjusting the synthesis conditions [4,5].

The heteroatom doping strategy is recognized as one of the effective methods to modulate the performance of CDs [6]. This approach involves selecting appropriate precursors to introduce different elements into the CDs during the synthesis, thereby enabling precise control over the energy band gap of the CDs. Consequently, the emission wavelength, quantum yield and other properties of CDs can be altered [7,8]. Nitrogen (N) is one of the most commonly used elements in CDs doping. When individual nitrogen atoms are doped into CDs, the unpaired electrons of nitrogen atoms can serve as electron donors, facilitating the transition from the ground state to the lowest excited state of the carbon point electron and reducing the energy required for this transition [9–11]. Furthermore, the introduction of nitrogen-containing functional groups, such as amide groups, onto the surface of CDs can enhance their radiation conversion ability and significantly improve the quantum yield. The polychromatic emission of the multicolor tunable nitrogen-doped CDs synthesized by Wei et al. with high quantum yield is primarily attributed to their large size distribution, sp2 conjugated structure and the presence of amino groups on the surface [12]. Previous studies have indicated, that most with solvent effects on CDs are mostly due to changes in their surface state. It is noteworthy that in addition to nitrogen doping, the presence of sulfur (S) can significantly alter the performance of CDs. For example, Gao et al. synthesized S-doped multicolor luminescent CDs. Through characterization and comparison, they found that S doping can significantly increase the content of graphitic nitrogen in CDs, which subsequently thereby leads to a red shift in fluorescence [13]. While numerous studies have been conducted to prepare multicolor tunable photoluminescence (PL) CDs with solvent effects, few CDs can cover the entire visible spectrum, which greatly limits the application scope of CDs [14]. When CDs are in high concentration or solid state, they often exhibit aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) phenomenon which is similar to the properties of organic molecules [15]. This phenomenon greatly limits the use of CDs in optical functional devices and other fields. One effective strategy to overcome this limitation is to use polymers to disperse CDs or immobilize them in various gels [16,17], which can inhibit the occurrence of ACQ phenomenon, promote the luminescence of CDs in the solid state, and enable the use of CDs in multiple fields such as monochrome LED lights [18–21]. In this work, we combined alkaline lignin and o-phenylenediamine for the first time and employ one-step solvothermal method to synthesize N, S-CDs with solvent effect. By simply changing the solvent in which N, S-CDs are dissolved, we observed that the fluorescence emission of CDs to gradually red-shift as the polarity of the solvent increases, thereby achieving tunable luminescence of N, S-CDs across the full spectrum of red, orange, yellow, green, cyan, and blue. It is convenient to create three single-color LED lamps by mixing N, S-CDs with PVA aqueous solution to achieve red, yellow and green solid-state film and coating them onto LED lamp beads. Furthermore, N, S-CDs can be used as bioluminescence probes for targeted positioning of cell organelle-lysosome and detection of heme.

Alkaline lignin, o-phenylenediamine (O-PDA), anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), n-Hexane (n-Hex), ethyl acetate (EA), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), formamide (FA), water (H2O), and acetic acid (AA) were all sourced from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used are analytical grade and do not require further refinement.

Test methods (see Scheme 1 ) and instruments are as follows: UV–Vis absorption spectra (Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer). Fluorescence spectra (Horiba FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer). Time-resolved luminescence decay curve (Fluorocube-01, JY-IBH, Horiba). TEM (JEM-2100F). X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+). Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (Nicolet 380). Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope, FE-SEM (Gemini300).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.516 g (0.001 mol) alkaline lignin and 0.216 g o-phenylenediamine (O-PD) (0.002 mol) were ground and mixed in 20 mL ethanol solution, and ultrasonicated for 1 min to dispersed evenly, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) lined autoclave and continuously heated in an electric blower drying oven at 200 °C for 6 h. Upon reaction completion, the mixture was cooled to ambient temperature, and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filter to remove large particles. The obtained mixture was purified and dialyzed continuously for 24 h. Finally, the N, S-CDs, which is a kind of the reddish-brown powder, were obtained after dried at 60 °C in a vacuum drying. Synthesis of L-CDs using alkaline lignin as the raw material and the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of different solvent of N, S-CDs solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The N, S-CDs were dissolved in n-hex, EA, DMF, EtOH, FA, H2O, and AA to prepare seven different solutions of N, S-CDs (each with a concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of N, S-CDs/PVA composite film
ce:section.ce:para: A 20 wt% aqueous solution of PVA was prepared. The homogeneous N, S-CDs/PVA gel solution was prepared by mixing 1 mL N, S-CDs solutions (1.0 mg mL−1) which used DMF, formamide, and AA as solvents with 4 mL of the PVA solution. Subsequently, the three gel mixtures were evenly spread onto three identical molds and cured for 24 h to obtain the N, S-CDs/PVA composite films.

A549 cells were cultured into a 96-well plate containing Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, Gibco™) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco™) and 1 % antibiotic-antimycotic solution (Gibco™), and incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO₂ atmosphere The cytotoxicity of all samples was assessed using a cell counting kit (CCK-8). First, 90 μL of cells were added to each well, at a density of approximately 1 × 10⁴ cells and incubated for 24 h. Then, 10 μL of N, S-CDs samples were added to the culture medium, with final concentrations of 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg mL⁻1. After co-culturing for 24 h, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution was added to each well and incubated for an additional 1 h. Finally, cell viability was measured using a microplate reader (Thermo Scientific Multiskan FC) by determining the absorbance at 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging and colocalization assays of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Add N, S-CDs solution with a concentration of 50 μg/mL to the cells and incubate for 10 h, resulting in a final concentration of 10 μg/mL for N, S-CDs. Afterwards, wash the cells twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to remove any attached samples. Then, add Lyso-Tracker Red (λEx = 577 nm, λEm = 590 nm, Beyotime) and co-incubate for 10 min. Finally, use a confocal fluorescence microscope to record the fluorescence imaging of the cells at the desired excitation wavelength for real-time imaging and tracking studies.

By investigating different reactant molar ratios, reaction temperatures, and reaction times (as shown in Table S1), it was found that the optimal experimental conditions for this work are a molar ratio of 1:2, a temperature of 200 °C, and a reaction time of 6 h.

To investigate the microstructure of the synthetic N, S-CDs, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) were performed. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the TEM image of N, S-CDs reveals that they were particles with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, which is similar to the lattice spacing of the (100) plane of graphene [22]. As can be seen in Fig. 1(b), the average particle size of the N, S-CDs is 1.58 nm ± 1 nm. During the experimental investigation, FTIR spectral analysis was carried out on both the alkaline lignin treated with the same conditions and the prepared N, S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the O–H stretching vibration at 3436 cm⁻1, the CC stretching vibration at 1621 cm−1, the C–C stretching vibration at 1438 cm⁻1, and the C–O stretching vibration at 1143 cm⁻1 collectively indicate that some functional groups of N, S-CDs originate from the raw material alkaline lignin. The N–H stretching vibration peak at 1496 cm⁻1 confirmed the formation of amino groups on the surface of the N, S-CDs [23]. Additionally, the peak at 1276 cm⁻1 represents the C–N stretching vibration, the peak at 933 cm⁻1 represents the C–O–C stretching vibration, and the peak at 752 cm⁻1 represents the C–S stretching vibration. Compared to alkaline lignin, the surface of N, S-CDs exhibits a richer variety of functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, and ester groups [24]. The presence of these functional groups enhances the dispersibility of N, S-CDs in both water and organic solvents, making it easier for the N, S-CDs to dissolve in various solvents.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to reveal the elemental composition and functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs. The full-range XPS spectrum of N, S-CDs is shown in Fig. 1(d), with signals for C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p observed at approximately 285 eV, 399.7 eV, 531.4 eV, and 163.6 eV, respectively [25]. The contents of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p were 63.2 %, 10.5 %, 23.9 % and 2.49 %. The XPS high-resolution spectra of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur are presented in Fig. 1(e), (f), (g) and (h), In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum, signals corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, C–S and CO/CN were observed at 284.6 eV, 285.8 eV, 287.2 eV and 288.2 eV, respectively [26]. Similarly, in high-resolution N1s, the peaks at 400.5 eV, 399.72eV and 398.65 eV correspond to Graphitic-N, Pyrrolic N and Pyridinic N, respectively [27,28]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s also proves that nitrogen (N) is primarily present in the carbon core of N, S-CDs [29]. These results are consistent with those obtained from FTIR spectroscopy, both of which fully confirm the successful synthesis of N, S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic optical properties of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To thoroughly investigate the luminescent properties of the N, S-CDs, a series of optical characterizations were conducted. Fig. 2 (a) shows the UV absorption, optimal excitation, and emission spectra of the as-synthesized N, S-CDs. The UV absorption peak of N, S-CDs in the low-energy region exhibits a strong absorption peak at 294 nm, which primarily attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the CC or CN bonds in the sp2 regions of the carbon core [30,31]. Furthermore, the UV absorption peak at 436 nm were attributed to the electronic transition within the conjugated structure between the carbon core and –NH [32]. The excitonic absorption peak of N, S-CDs, observed at 430 nm, corresponds to the optimal excitation wavelength of 436 nm. The optimal emission wavelength of the N, S-CDs is 546 nm. Additionally, emission spectra were measured within a range of ±50 nm around the optimal excitation wavelength. By analyzing these emission spectra, the excitation-independence of the N, S-CDs was characterized.

By varying the pH of the N, S-CDs solution, it was observed that the main emission peak of N, S-CDs remained at 546 nm in both acidic and alkaline environments. However, new emission peaks appeared under strong acidic conditions, as depicted in Fig. 2(c) and Fig. S1. This is preliminarily ascribed to the protonation of amino functional groups in the N, S-CDs under acidic conditions. To further investigate this phenomenon, N, S-CDs were dissolved in concentrated hydrochloric acid, nitric acid, sulfuric acid, and phosphoric acid. It was observed that the emission of the N, S-CDs underwent similar changes, with significant red shifts, as shown in Fig. S2. As presented in Fig. 2(d), the fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CDs, fitted with a single exponential model, is 3.72 ns.

It is intriguing that the N, S-CDs exhibited a significant fluorescence emission peak shift when dissolved in seven different solvents with varying polarities (n-Hex, EA, DMF, EtOH, H2O, FA, and AA). Specifically, as the polarity of the solvent increased, the fluorescence emission peak of N, S-CDs shifted towards longer wavelengths (red shift). As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the emission peak positions of N, S-CDs in different solvents are presented. When N, S-CDs were dissolved in the non-polar solvent n-Hex, it exhibited blue light emission with an optimal emission wavelength of 383 nm. The optimal emission peak was at 512 nm in EA, shifted to 539 nm in DMF, to 546 nm in EtOH, to 560 nm in FA, to 579 nm in H2O, and to 706 nm in AA. This shift is accompanied by a change in the fluorescence color, displaying a full spectrum from blue to red. As shown in Fig. 3 (b), a notable observation is that there is a simple linear relationship between solvent polarity and the fluorescence emission wavelength of N, S-CDs, except AA. As the solvent polarity increases, the emission of N, S-CDs dissolved in various solvents shifts towards the red end of the spectrum. This phenomenon is attributed to the aggregated distribution of N, S-CDs in highly polar solvents. As depicted in Fig. S3, TEM images of N, S-CDs in various solvents are presented. After analyzing the dynamic light scattering (DLS) data of N, S-CDs in various solvents, it was observed that these carbon dots exhibited significantly different aggregation behaviors in solvents of different polarities. The overall trend indicated that as the polarity of the solvent increased, the degree of aggregation of N, S-CDs also increased. Furthermore, by comparing the fluorescence lifetime data in different solvents (as shown in Fig. S5), it was noted that the fluorescence lifetime decreased with the enhancement of solvent polarity. This phenomenon was primarily attributed to the increased hydrogen bonding in strongly polar protic solvents, which promoted the formation of aggregates of N, S-CDs to a certain extent within the solvent. Such aggregation altered the stacking structure between N, S-CDs nanoparticles, leading to the overlap of surface state energy levels, and thus affecting the distribution of energy levels and the process of electronic transitions. In conjunction with the preceding fluorescence emission and aggregation state analysis [3,33]. Referring to Table 1 for relevant solvent polarity and parameters (α represents the hydrogen-bond donating ability of the solvents, β indicates the hydrogen-bond accepting ability of the solvents) [10]. By comparing several solvents, it was observed that although acetic acid was a moderately polar solvent, it exhibited a relatively strong hydrogen-donating ability. Combined with the double emission peaks of N, S-CDs observed under acidic conditions (as shown in Fig. S1), it was preliminarily hypothesized that the significant red shift occurring in acetic acid was mainly due to the protonation of N, S-CDs in this protic solvent, which led to the formation of strong hydrogen bonds. A hydrogen bond is a special type of intermolecular or intramolecular interaction between a hydrogen atom and atoms like oxygen, fluorine, or nitrogen [33,34]. This interaction could alter the electron distribution of CDs, further stabilizing the excited state [35]. To confirm the validity of this hypothesis, N, S-CDs were dissolved in seven different solvents and then their UV–visible absorption spectra were tested (as shown in Fig. 3(C)). It can be found that in different solvents, the exciton absorption peaks of N, S-CDs in n-Hex, EA, DMF, EtOH, H2O and FA solvents did not change significantly. However, when dissolved in acetic acid solvent, new absorption peaks appeared at 539 nm and 635 nm. This is mainly because in AA, the –NH2 on the surface of N, S-CDs obtains hydrogen to form –NH3 +, which is protonated [36–38]. This protonation led to near-infrared emission of N, S-CDs in AA solution. Combined with the above analysis, it can be found that N, S-CDs have significant solvent effects, and their fluorescence emission characteristics can be controlled by changing the polarity of the solvent. This provides the possibility for the application of N, S-CDs in optical sensing, imaging and display technology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N, S-CDs/PVA composite materials and solid-state thin film luminescence
ce:section.ce:para: Optical photographs of the N, S-CDs/PVA films under daylight (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom) are shown in Fig. S6.

As shown in Figs. S7, N, S-CDs exhibited distinct fluorescence emissions of red, yellow, and green when dissolved in AA, FA, and DMF, respectively. The fluorescence emission observed in the PVA film containing N, S-CDs is attributed to the abundant presence of PVA chains surrounding the nanoparticles. These PVA chains act as barriers in the aggregated state, effectively inhibiting π-π∗ stacking and thus preventing aggregation-induced quenching of the N, S-CDs’ fluorescence [39,40]. By initially dispersing the N, S-CDs in suitable solvents and then mixing them uniformly with PVA gel, the nanoparticles are able to retain their respective states even as the solvents evaporate. This unique approach allows the N, S-CDs dissolved in AA, DMF, and FA to exhibit three distinct fluorescence emissions when incorporated into the same PVA substrate [41]. Fig. 4(a) shows the SEM image of pure PVA film without N, S-CDs doping, exhibiting a smooth and uniform microstructure. In contrast, Fig. 5(b), (c), and (d) display SEM images of PVA composite films doped with N, S-CDs dissolved in three different solvents. Upon visual inspection, it is evident that the aggregation states of N, S-CDs within these composite films are distinct. This variation highlights the difference in the dissolution states of N, S-CDs in different solvents, which subsequently influence their aggregation morphology within the PVA matrix.

After applying PVA/N, S-CDs composite film on the 365 nm LED chip, the resulting light bulbs emitted monochromatic light in red, yellow, and green, as depicted in Fig. 5(e), (f) and (g). The corresponding CIE coordinates were (0.687, 0.3128) for red, (0.4433, 0.5106) for yellow, and (0.4109, 0.5443) for green. These results underscore the significant potential and promising applications of N, S-CDs in the field of solid-state lighting and optical devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hemoglobin fluorescent probes and lysosomal staining
ce:section.ce:para: Given the excellent solubility in water and optical properties of N, S-CDs, they can be utilized in an aqueous solution for specific detection of hemoglobin (the concentration of the added amino acids and biological macromolecule solutions is 2∗10−3 mol/L). As illustrated in Fig. 5(a) and (b), even in the presence of amino acids or other biomolecules, hemoglobin demonstrates notable fluorescence quenching with N, S-CDs. This observation suggests that N, S-CDs hold potential for practical biological detection and recognition of hemoglobin, making them a valuable tool in biosensing and biomedical applications. In Fig. 6 (c), as the concentration of hemoglobin is incremented from 0 μM to 120 μM, a corresponding increase in the fluorescence quenching of N, S-CDs is observed. The linear regression analysis shown in Fig. 6 (d) reveals a strong linear correlation between the hemoglobin concentration and the degree of fluorescence quenching. Within this concentration range, the linear regression equation is expressed as y = 112.4X + 2963.8, with a linear fitting coefficient of R2 = 0.995 [42]. Using UV–visible absorption spectra and time-resolved fluorescence decay curves, the fluorescence response mechanism of N, S-CDs to hemoglobin can be further explored. As illustrated in Fig. 6 (e), the fluorescence lifetimes of N, S-CDs (τN, S-CDs) and N, S-CDs after addition of hemoglobin (τN, S-CDs + H) are both measured to be 2.54 ns, resulting in a ratio of τ N, S-CDs/ τ N, S-CDs + H = 1. This indicates that the fluorescence lifetime remains unchanged upon the addition of hemoglobin. However, as depicted in Fig. 6 (f), when comparing the UV–visible absorption spectra of N, S-CDs and N, S-CDs with hemoglobin, it was evident that, new absorption peaks emerged upon the addition of hemoglobin. This observation, combining with the time-resolved fluorescence decay curves, confirms that hemoglobin induced static fluorescence quenching in N, S-CDs. This was likely due to a chelation interaction between hemoglobin and the surface of N, S-CDs, forming a non-fluorescent ground-state complex [43,44].This interaction explains the observed fluorescence quenching and the emergence of new absorption peaks in the UV–visible absorption spectra [45]. These findings provide valuable insights into the mechanism of N, S-CDs' fluorescence response to hemoglobin and further support their potential for practical biological detection and recognition applications.

Using N, S-CDs for cytotoxicity testing on A549 cells validates their potential for cellular localization imaging, as shown in Fig. S8. It can be observed that, after incubating with N, S-CDs solutions at different concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg mL−1) for 24 h, the viability of A549 cells remains consistently above 90 %. This indicates that N, S-CDs exhibit minimal cytotoxicity towards the cells, with negligible impact on their survival. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was used to assess the staining capability of N, S-CDs on cells, The specificity of N, S-CD for targeting particular organelles was determined by separately labeling cellular organelles, such as mitochondria, lysosomes, and lipid droplets with specific markers. Through colocalization analysis with LTR dye, it was observed that N, S-CDs exhibited excellent targeting capability towards lysosomes in A549 cells, which was distinct from mitochondria and deep red lipid droplets. Specifically, N, S-CDs showed significant targeting to lysosomes, as demonstrated in Fig. 6 (a), with a colocalization coefficient of 0.86. This indicated that the localization of N, S-CDs in A549 cells was highly consistent with that of LTR dye, primarily aggregating in lysosomes rather than other organelles such as mitochondria or lipid droplets. The high colocalization coefficient suggested the potential application of N, S-CDs in bioimaging and targeted therapy, highlighting their value as a versatile tool for cellular research and therapeutic applications.

Three sets of experiments were conducted to investigate the ability of N, S-CDs to specifically recognize and be effectively applied for biological cell imaging. The first group was a blank control, where cells were stained exclusively with N, S-CDs. In the second and third groups, cells were pre-treated with heme solutions at concentrations of 60 μM and 120 μM, respectively, prior to staining with N, S-CDs. The results, as depicted in Fig. 6 (d), revealed that as the heme concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of lysosomes in A549 cells decreased. This observation suggests that N, S-CDs can effectively visualize the presence of heme in lysosomes, thereby demonstrating their potential for the specific and quantitative detection of heme in biological cells.

In summary, this paper reports the synthesis of sulfur-nitrogen co-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) via a simple one-step solvothermal method, utilizing alkaline lignin and o-phenylenediamine as precursors in anhydrous ethanol as solvent. The synthesized N, S-CDs displayed a uniform particle size distribution, emitted yellow fluorescence with an emission wavelength of 546 nm, and exhibited excellent excitation independence and pH stability. When dispersed in solvents of varying polarities, such as n-Hex, EA, DMF, EtOH, FA, H2O and AA, the fluorescence emission of N, S-CDs showed a red shift as solvent polarity increased. The presence of numerous amino groups on the surface of N, S-CDs led to their protonation in AA, and the synergistic effect of polarity and hydrogen bonds facilitated near-infrared fluorescence emission. Consequently, N, S-CDs were capable of emitting across the full color spectrum, including red, orange, yellow, green, cyan, and blue. The N, S-CDs were dissolved in three solvents (DMF, FA, and AA), dispersed in PVA aqueous solutions, and naturally dried to form films. These films emitted red, yellow, and green colors, demonstrating their potential to adjust the emission of monochrome LED lamps and highlighting their potential in optical functional devices. Furthermore, N, S-CDs exhibited low biotoxicity and excellent optical properties in water, making them suitable as fluorescent probes for heme and enabling targeted lysosome staining in biological imaging. Overall, the unique properties and versatility of N, S-CDs underscore their potential in various applications, including optical devices, biological imaging, and diagnostics.
Jing Zhu: Writing – original draft. Songfang Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Ruifang Guan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Hao Zhang: Writing – review & editing.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62271231) and the Natural Science Foundation of China (52411540236).
